title: Listening - Part 1
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear three different extracts. For questions 1 – 6, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear. There are two questions for each extract.

extracts.context: You hear two colleagues, a man and a woman, discussing a professional development course.
extracts.audio_script: Woman: So, how are you finding the leadership seminar? Man: Honestly? [1]It’s a bit of a mixed bag. The theory is sound enough, I suppose, but the delivery is incredibly dry.[/1] I find my mind wandering after the first twenty minutes. Woman: That's a pity. I've heard the facilitator is quite a big name in the industry. Man: He might be, but he spends half the time name-dropping and the other half reminiscing about his early days. It’s hardly the cutting-edge insight I was promised. Woman: Well, [2]the networking aspect alone should make it worth your while, shouldn't it?[/2] Everyone who’s anyone in project management is there. Man: I guess so, though I’ve always found those forced interactions a bit awkward. Still, I did have an interesting chat with someone from the Birmingham office during the coffee break. Woman: See? That’s where the real value lies, not in the PowerPoint slides.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: So, how are you finding the leadership seminar?

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Honestly? It’s a bit of a mixed bag. The theory is sound enough, I suppose, but the delivery is incredibly dry. I find my mind wandering after the first twenty minutes.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: See? That’s where the real value lies, not in the PowerPoint slides.

questions.question: What is the man's main criticism of the seminar?
questions.explanation: He says the 'delivery is incredibly dry' and his 'mind wanders', indicating a lack of engagement with the style.

questions.question: The woman suggests that the man should focus on...

options.B: the quality of the course materials.

questions.explanation: She mentions the 'networking aspect' and implies that meeting people is 'where the real value lies'.

extracts.context: You hear two friends from Ireland, Emily and Connor, talking about their city's new bike-sharing scheme.
extracts.audio_script: Man: Have you tried those new city bikes yet, Emily? Woman: I have, actually. Though I must say, [3]the app is a bit of a nightmare to navigate when you're in a hurry[/3]. It took me ages just to unlock the thing. Man: Really? I found it quite intuitive. Mind you, the bikes themselves are a bit heavy, aren't they? Trying to get up the hill by the cathedral was a proper workout. Woman: Tell me about it! But [4]it’s brilliant not having to worry about your own bike getting pinched[/4] while you're in the shops. Man: That’s a fair point. And it’s definitely cheaper than getting the bus into the centre every day. Woman: It is, though I think they need more docking stations out in the suburbs. If you don't live within a mile of the clock tower, you're out of luck. Man: True, it’s early days yet, I suppose.

questions.question: What was Emily's experience with the system's software?

options.B: She struggled to use it efficiently.
options.C: It failed to work on her specific device.

questions.explanation: She calls the app a 'nightmare to navigate' and says it 'took ages' to unlock the bike.

questions.question: What do both speakers agree is a significant advantage of the scheme?

options.A: The physical health benefits of cycling.
options.B: The low cost compared to public transport.
options.C: The reduction in security concerns for cyclists.

questions.explanation: Emily mentions not worrying about the bike 'getting pinched' (stolen), and Connor agrees it is a 'fair point'.

extracts.context: You hear two young adults discussing their experiences with remote work.
extracts.audio_script: Man: I don't know about you, Mia, but I'm starting to find working from my kitchen table a bit soul-destroying. Woman: Really? I’m the opposite. I feel like [5]I’ve reclaimed hours of my life now that the commute is out of the window[/5]. I’m much more productive. Man: I get that, but [6]I find the boundaries between 'home' and 'work' are just completely blurred now[/6]. I’m checking emails at 9 PM because the laptop is just... there. Woman: You have to be disciplined! I set a hard 'power-off' time at 5:30. Man: I wish it were that simple. My boss seems to think 'remote' means 'available 24/7'. Woman: That’s a management issue, not a remote work issue. Maybe you should have a word? Man: Perhaps. I just miss the office banter, you know? The kitchen table doesn't have much of a personality.

questions.question: Why does the woman prefer working from home?

options.A: She enjoys the silence of her home environment.
options.B: She has more free time due to less travel.
options.C: She feels more comfortable in a domestic setting.

questions.explanation: She mentions she has 'reclaimed hours' of her life because the 'commute is out of the window'.

questions.question: What is the man's main difficulty with the current arrangement?

options.A: He finds it hard to separate his professional and private life.
options.B: He lacks the necessary equipment to work effectively.
options.C: He is unable to complete his tasks on time.

questions.explanation: He explicitly states the boundaries are 'completely blurred' and he finds himself working late because his work is always present.
